chunks.title: Cloud service models
chunks.text: Cloud services come in three layers. IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) gives you VMs, storage, and networks — you manage the OS and everything above it. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs. PaaS (Platform as a Service) abstracts the OS — you deploy code, the platform handles scaling and patching. Examples: Heroku, App Engine. SaaS (Software as a Service) delivers a finished application. Examples: Gmail, Salesforce. FaaS (Functions as a Service) runs single functions on demand, billed per invocation.

chunks.title: Virtualization and containers
chunks.text: Virtualization runs multiple virtual machines on one physical host, each with its own kernel — strong isolation but heavyweight (gigabytes of RAM per VM). Containers (Docker) share the host kernel and isolate via namespaces and cgroups — far lighter (megabytes) and start in seconds. A Docker image is a layered filesystem; a container is a running instance. Kubernetes orchestrates many containers across a cluster, scheduling them, restarting failed ones, and load-balancing.

chunks.title: AWS core services
chunks.text: AWS, the largest cloud provider, organizes services by category. Compute: EC2 (VMs), Lambda (FaaS), ECS/EKS (containers). Storage: S3 (object storage, infinitely scalable), EBS (block storage for EC2), EFS (file shares). Database: RDS (managed SQL), DynamoDB (key-value), Aurora (high-perf SQL). Networking: VPC (private network), Route 53 (DNS), CloudFront (CDN). IAM controls who can do what. CloudWatch handles monitoring and logs.

chunks.title: Scalability patterns
chunks.text: Vertical scaling (scale up) means giving one server more CPU/RAM — simple but capped by hardware. Horizontal scaling (scale out) means adding more servers behind a load balancer — limitless in principle but requires statelessness or distributed state. Caching layers (Redis, Memcached) reduce database load. Read replicas spread read traffic. Sharding splits data across multiple databases by key. Eventually-consistent stores trade strict consistency for availability.

chunks.title: Microservices
chunks.text: Microservices break a large application into small, independently deployable services that communicate over the network (HTTP or message queue). Each service owns its data and can use a different language or database. Benefits: independent deploys, team autonomy, targeted scaling. Costs: network calls are slow and unreliable, distributed transactions are hard, observability needs investment (tracing, structured logs). Microservices are not a free lunch; a monolith is fine until it isn't.

chunks.title: Serverless and FaaS
chunks.text: Serverless platforms (AWS Lambda, Cloud Functions) run code only when triggered — by HTTP request, queue message, or schedule. You pay per invocation and execution time, not for idle servers. Cold starts (first invocation latency) are a real concern; pinning a minimum number of warm instances helps. Serverless fits event-driven workloads and bursty traffic. It's poor for long-running compute, large state, or sub-millisecond latency.

chunks.title: Cloud cost management
chunks.text: Cloud bills can balloon without discipline. Reserved instances (1-3 year commitment) save 30-70% vs on-demand. Spot/preemptible instances are very cheap but can be evicted — fine for batch jobs with checkpointing. Right-sizing finds the smallest instance type that still works. Auto-scaling cuts capacity during off-peak. Egress (data leaving the cloud) is expensive; keep services in the same region. Tags + cost reports let you attribute spend to teams.

chunks.title: DevOps and infrastructure as code
chunks.text: Infrastructure as code (IaC) declares cloud resources in version-controlled files — Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi. Spinning up an identical staging environment becomes a `terraform apply` instead of clicking through a console. Configuration management tools (Ansible, Chef, Puppet) handle in-place server config. CI/CD pipelines deploy code after passing tests. Observability — metrics, logs, traces — closes the loop, letting you detect and debug production issues quickly.

chunks.title: Kubernetes pods and deployments
chunks.text: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit — one or more containers sharing a network namespace and volumes. A Deployment declares a desired number of pod replicas and handles rolling updates. A Service exposes pods to other workloads with a stable IP and DNS name. Labels and selectors connect them. ReplicaSets (created by Deployments) actually maintain the pod count. Kubernetes is declarative — you describe the desired state and the controller reconciles.

chunks.title: Helm charts
chunks.text: Helm is Kubernetes's package manager. A chart bundles a related set of K8s manifests with templating (Go templates) and configurable values. `helm install myapp ./mychart -f prod.yaml` deploys with environment-specific values. Charts are versioned and can be published to chart repositories. The standard way to share K8s applications — most major projects (Prometheus, Grafana, ingress controllers) publish official charts.

chunks.title: Container registries
chunks.text: A container registry stores Docker images. Docker Hub is the public default; private registries include AWS ECR, GCP GCR/Artifact Registry, Azure ACR, GitHub Container Registry, Harbor. Best practices: tag images with semver + commit hash, scan for vulnerabilities, use private registries for proprietary code, set lifecycle rules to delete old images. Pull-through caches reduce egress and improve build times.

chunks.title: Docker Compose
chunks.text: Docker Compose defines multi-container applications in a YAML file: services, networks, volumes. `docker compose up` brings everything up; `docker compose down` tears it down. Compose is the standard for local dev — your app + database + cache + queue in one command. For production, Kubernetes is more powerful but Compose remains popular for single-host deployments and CI environments.

chunks.title: Service mesh
chunks.text: A service mesh (Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect) handles service-to-service communication concerns — mutual TLS, retries, timeouts, circuit breaking, observability — without changing application code. Each pod runs a sidecar proxy (Envoy in Istio) that intercepts traffic. Pros: uniform policy enforcement, rich telemetry. Cons: complexity, resource overhead, additional failure mode. Worth it past ~50 services; overkill below.

chunks.title: Cloud-native patterns
chunks.text: Cloud-native software is designed for elastic, distributed, automatable environments. Patterns: stateless services (easy to scale), horizontal scaling first, externalized config (env vars, config maps), health checks, graceful shutdown, observability built in. The 12-factor app methodology codifies these. Containers and Kubernetes are typical implementations but the patterns predate them.

chunks.title: 12-factor app
chunks.text: The 12-factor methodology: I. Codebase tracked in version control. II. Dependencies declared and isolated. III. Config in environment. IV. Backing services as attached resources. V. Strict build/release/run separation. VI. Processes are stateless. VII. Port binding. VIII. Concurrency via process model. IX. Disposability (fast startup, graceful shutdown). X. Dev/prod parity. XI. Logs as event streams. XII. Admin processes as one-off processes.

chunks.title: Stateful vs stateless services
chunks.text: Stateless services hold no client-specific state between requests — any instance can serve any request, easy to scale and replace. Stateful services (databases, caches, session stores) need stable identity and persistent storage. Kubernetes handles them with StatefulSets, which give pods stable names and persistent volume claims. Most apps push state into managed services (RDS, ElastiCache) and keep their own services stateless.

chunks.title: Storage classes: object, block, file
chunks.text: Object storage (S3, GCS) stores opaque blobs accessed by key, infinitely scalable, eventual consistency on metadata historically. Block storage (EBS, GCP persistent disk) is raw disk attached to a single VM, supports any filesystem, like a hard drive. File storage (EFS, Filestore) is shared filesystem accessible by many VMs, supports POSIX semantics. Choose by access pattern: archives → object, databases → block, shared writes → file.

chunks.title: CDN architecture
chunks.text: A CDN runs hundreds of edge locations globally. When a user requests content, DNS or anycast routes them to the nearest edge. The edge serves cached content directly; on miss, it fetches from the origin and caches the result. Modern CDNs also run user code at the edge (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge) for personalization without origin round-trips. TLS termination at the edge offloads work from origins.

chunks.title: Multi-region deployment
chunks.text: Deploying across multiple regions improves latency and availability. Active-passive: traffic to primary, failover to secondary on outage. Active-active: traffic distributed by geography or weighted DNS. Challenges: data replication latency, conflict resolution, cost. Most apps don't need multi-region until they have many users globally or strict availability SLAs. Don't underestimate operational complexity.

chunks.title: Disaster recovery
chunks.text: DR planning defines two metrics: RTO (Recovery Time Objective — how long can we be down?) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective — how much data can we lose?). Strategies (cheapest to most expensive): backup and restore (RTO hours), pilot light (minimal infra always running), warm standby (full but scaled-down replica), multi-region active-active (near-zero RTO/RPO). Test the DR plan regularly — untested DR is no DR.

chunks.title: Backup strategies
chunks.text: The 3-2-1 rule: keep at least 3 copies of data, on 2 different media, with 1 offsite. Cloud backups (AWS Backup, Azure Backup) automate snapshots and cross-region replication. Point-in-time recovery rolls back to any moment. Backup encryption protects data at rest. Critical: test restores periodically — many organisations discover their backups are corrupt only during a real incident.

chunks.title: Managed databases
chunks.text: Managed databases (RDS, Cloud SQL, Azure SQL) handle provisioning, patches, backups, failover, read replicas. Trade-offs vs self-managed: less control, vendor lock-in, higher cost; but no 3am calls. DBaaS variants: serverless (Aurora Serverless, Cosmos DB) scales automatically; planet-scale (Spanner, CockroachDB) handles geo-distributed transactions. Most workloads start managed and consider self-managed only for unusual requirements.

chunks.title: Managed Kubernetes
chunks.text: EKS (AWS), GKE (Google), AKS (Azure) manage the K8s control plane. You operate worker nodes (or use serverless modes — EKS Fargate, GKE Autopilot). Benefits: no etcd ops, automatic upgrades, integrated cloud features (load balancers, storage classes). Drawbacks: still complex, costs add up. For small workloads, simpler PaaS (Cloud Run, App Runner) often beats Kubernetes.

chunks.title: Cloud IAM
chunks.text: Cloud IAM controls who (users, services, federated identities) can do what (actions) on which resources. AWS IAM uses policies with effect/action/resource/condition. Best practices: least privilege (start deny-all, grant explicitly), no long-lived access keys (use roles), MFA on root and admin accounts, audit periodically. IAM misconfigurations are responsible for many cloud breaches.

chunks.title: API Gateway
chunks.text: An API Gateway is the single entry point for client requests to backend services. Responsibilities: routing, authentication, rate limiting, request/response transformation, caching, monitoring. AWS API Gateway, GCP API Gateway, Kong, Tyk. Used in microservices to provide a clean external API while internal services evolve. Can be a bottleneck if not horizontally scaled.

chunks.title: Message queues
chunks.text: Message queues decouple producers from consumers. SQS is a managed queue (FIFO or standard). SNS is pub/sub (one publisher, many subscribers). Kafka is a distributed log — durable, ordered, partitioned, supports replay. RabbitMQ is feature-rich (routing, dead letter queues, priorities). Choose by throughput needs and feature requirements. Always plan for poison messages (dead letter queues) and idempotent consumers.

chunks.title: Stream processing
chunks.text: Stream processing handles continuous data flows in real time. Kafka Streams runs distributed processing inline with Kafka. Apache Flink supports complex event processing with exactly-once guarantees. Spark Streaming does micro-batches. AWS Kinesis Data Streams is the managed AWS option. Use cases: fraud detection, IoT telemetry, real-time analytics, ETL pipelines.

chunks.title: Data lakes and warehouses
chunks.text: A data lake stores raw data (often Parquet/JSON in object storage) cheaply for later analysis — schema-on-read. A data warehouse stores cleaned, structured, query-optimized data — schema-on-write. Modern lakehouses (Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi) combine: cheap storage of lakes with ACID transactions and metadata of warehouses. Pipelines (Airflow, Dagster, dbt) move data from sources to lake/warehouse for analytics.

chunks.title: Cloud data warehouses
chunks.text: BigQuery (Google), Snowflake, Redshift (AWS), Synapse (Azure) are managed analytical databases optimized for OLAP. Columnar storage and massive parallelism let them scan petabytes in seconds. Pricing models differ: BigQuery per query bytes, Snowflake per warehouse minute, Redshift per node hour. Separation of storage and compute is the modern norm — store once, spin up compute as needed.

chunks.title: Cloud monitoring
chunks.text: Cloud monitoring collects metrics, logs, traces from infrastructure and applications. CloudWatch (AWS), Stackdriver/Cloud Operations (GCP), Azure Monitor are the native services. Third-party: Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace, Honeycomb, Grafana Cloud. Alerts fire on threshold breaches. Dashboards visualize state. Cost can balloon — log volume and metric cardinality are the usual culprits.

chunks.title: Distributed tracing
chunks.text: A distributed trace tracks one request as it flows through many services, recording timing and metadata at each hop. Each operation is a span; spans share a trace ID. Propagation injects the IDs into request headers (W3C Trace Context). Tools: Jaeger, Tempo, Zipkin, Honeycomb, X-Ray. OpenTelemetry is the vendor-neutral instrumentation standard. Tracing reveals which service is the bottleneck in a complex call graph.

chunks.title: Chaos engineering
chunks.text: Chaos engineering injects controlled failures into production to verify resilience. Netflix's Chaos Monkey randomly terminates instances; Chaos Kong takes out entire regions. Game days are scheduled chaos exercises. The point isn't to break things — it's to find weaknesses before customers do. Modern tools: Gremlin, AWS Fault Injection Simulator, LitmusChaos. Start in staging; graduate to production with kill switches ready.

chunks.title: Multi-cloud and hybrid
chunks.text: Multi-cloud spreads workloads across multiple providers — for availability, negotiation leverage, regulatory needs. Hybrid combines on-premise and cloud — useful during migration or for data residency. Costs: complexity, no single skill set covers everything, integration overhead. Worth it for specific reasons (vendor risk, compliance) but most teams overcomplicate. Single-cloud is fine for most.

chunks.title: GitOps
chunks.text: GitOps treats Git as the single source of truth for infrastructure and applications. Tools like ArgoCD or Flux watch a repository and reconcile the cluster to match the declared state. Benefits: every change is reviewed and auditable, rollback is git revert, no out-of-band kubectl applies. Pull-based (cluster reads git) is more secure than push-based (CI pushes to cluster) because the cluster doesn't expose deploy credentials.
